questions.prompt: Complete: "Children ___ cross the road without looking both ways. It's dangerous."
questions.explanation: Crossing without looking is forbidden and dangerous, so we use mustn't: children mustn't cross without looking. "should" gives soft advice (not a prohibition), "must" would make the dangerous action obligatory, and "don't have to" means it is simply optional.

questions.prompt: Complete: "It's a free event, so you ___ buy a ticket."
questions.explanation: A free event means buying a ticket is not necessary: you don't have to buy one. "must" and "have to" would make buying obligatory (the opposite), and "mustn't" would forbid buying — neither fits a free, optional entry.

questions.prompt: Complete: "You look pale. I think you ___ go home and rest."
questions.explanation: This is friendly advice based on how someone looks, so we use should + base verb: you should go home. "must to" adds a wrong to, bare "have" cannot express advice here, and "mustn't" would forbid going home — the opposite of the intended meaning.

options.text: She doesn't have to come if she's busy.
He should to call his parents more often.
You mustn't take photos in this gallery.

questions.explanation: The error is in the third option: should is a modal and is followed by the bare infinitive with no to — He should call his parents. The first option (must + base) and fourth option (mustn't + base) are correct, as is the second (doesn't have to + base).

questions.prompt:
Read the signs: "NO PARKING 8–10 a.m. · SPEED LIMIT 30 mph · HELMETS RECOMMENDED"
Which modal best completes: "You ___ wear a helmet — it is not legally required but is strongly advised."
questions.explanation: "Recommended" means it is a good idea but not a law, so should fits: you should wear a helmet. "must" and "have to" express legal obligation (the sign says it is NOT legally required), and "mustn't" would forbid wearing a helmet — completely the wrong meaning.

questions.prompt: Complete: "When I was ten, I ___ do the washing-up every evening — that was my chore."
questions.explanation: "When I was ten" places the obligation in the past. The past form of must / have to is had to: I had to do the washing-up. "must" has no past form of its own, "have to" is the present form, and "must to" wrongly adds to after must.
